results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call explicitly targets consortia of academe, civil society, and community-based organizations, which is an exhaustive list that does not include UN agencies or intergovernmental organizations.
results.classification_summary: The GOJUST Window 3 grant facility targets consortia of academic institutions, civil society organizations, and community-based organizations working on access to justice in the Philippines. The eligible applicant categories are exhaustively defined as these civil-society and academic entities, with no mention of UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, or multilateral institutions. The ILO, as a UN specialized agency and IGO, does not fall within the eligible categories and is therefore ineligible. The call is otherwise active with a future EOI deadline of 29 May 2026 and a final proposal deadline of 31 August 2026, offering up to USD 250,000 per consortium.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly targets 'academe, civil society, and community-based organizations' operating within or near Justice Zones. This list is exhaustive and does not include UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, or multilateral institutions. The ILO as a UN IGO/specialized agency does not qualify.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page explicitly refers to a 'Grant Facility' and states consortia 'may receive support of up to $250,000', indicating a non-reimbursable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Consortia of three members may receive up to $250,000, which exceeds the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The EOI deadline is May 29, 2026, and the final proposal submission deadline is August 31, 2026. Both are after today's date of 2026-05-25. The EOI deadline (May 29) is the first required step and is still in the future.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page describes a single specific call (GOJUST Window 3) with an explicit EOI deadline of May 29, 2026, orientation sessions June 3-5, and final proposal deadline August 31, 2026. An active application link is provided.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call funds initiatives to strengthen coordination for access to justice for women, Indigenous Peoples, and vulnerable groups — programmatic technical assistance and capacity building that an ILO team could in principle deliver. However, ILO is ineligible on entity-type grounds.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The page describes a Call for Proposals grant facility, not a procurement tender or RFP.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page does not mention co-financing requirements; full guidelines are available via an external link not captured in the content.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No mention of specific accreditation requirements with any external body.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call requires consortia of three members but no specific leadership/lead-entity rules are detailed in the page content.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call targets organizations working within or near 'Justice Zones' in the Philippines, indicating geographic restriction to Philippines-based entities.

results.title: EU GOJUST Access to Justice Grant Facility (Window 3) – Philippines
results.deadline_text: May 29: Deadline for Expression of Interest (EOI)
theme: Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work
eligible_activities: Strengthening coordination and collaboration between academe, civil society, and community-based organizations
Initiatives to make the justice system work for women, Indigenous Peoples, and those in vulnerable situations
Work within or near designated Justice Zones
results.proposal_summary: The European Union GOJUST Access to Justice Grant Facility (Window 3) is an EU-funded open call in the Philippines aimed at supporting consortia of three members drawn from academic institutions, civil society organizations, and community-based organizations. The grant supports initiatives that strengthen coordination and collaboration to improve access to justice for women, Indigenous Peoples, and vulnerable groups within designated Justice Zones. Consortia may receive up to USD 250,000 in non-reimbursable grant funding. The first mandatory step is submission of an Expression of Interest by 29 May 2026, followed by orientation sessions on 3–5 June 2026 and a final proposal deadline of 31 August 2026. Eligible applicants are restricted to organizations working within or near Justice Zones in the Philippines; UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not among the eligible applicant types.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call addresses rights of vulnerable groups (women, Indigenous Peoples) and access to justice, which have some relevance to ILO's fundamental rights mandate, the primary focus is on judicial system reform and legal access rather than core ILO themes of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

However, this is a call for conference session proposals, not a grant call.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call does not fund any programmatic activity; it solicits voluntary session contributions to a conference. There is no grant objective to assess for ILO implementability.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: No procurement or tender elements are present; this is a call for voluntary session contributions to a conference.

results.title: EU Community of Practice on Peace Mediation 2026: Call for Voluntary Session Proposals
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Submission of voluntary session proposals using the provided template
EU Community of Practice on Peace Mediation event in Brussels (20-21 October 2026)

eligible_activities: Panel discussions
Workshops
Interactive dialogues on peace mediation topics
funding_restrictions: No financial grant is available; this is a voluntary call for session proposals only
results.proposal_summary: The European External Action Service (EEAS) is calling for voluntary session proposals for the 2026 EU Community of Practice on Peace Mediation (CoP), its flagship annual event bringing together government representatives, policymakers, mediation practitioners, experts, and civil society from around the world. The event will take place in Brussels on 20-21 October 2026. This is not a grant call — no financial funding is offered. Session conveners are invited to submit proposals for panels, workshops, or interactive dialogues by 15 June 2026 using the provided template, with the aim of sharing lessons learned and best practices on peace mediation. No co-financing, accreditation, or geographic restrictions apply.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call concerns peace mediation and conflict resolution, which is tangentially related to ILO's work in crisis contexts (decent work in crisis/post-crisis situations), but has no direct connection to ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue, and offers no financial grant.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO eligibility not established: the call invites 'organisations meeting the eligibility criteria' without explicitly naming UN agencies, international organisations, or IGOs as eligible entity types. The detailed guidelines PDF was not retrievable, so the entity-type list cannot be confirmed to include the ILO. Under the exhaustive-list rule, eligibility cannot be assumed from silence when the page uses the phrase 'organisations meeting the eligibility criteria' without further specification visible in the provided content.
results.classification_summary: This is an active EU Delegation to Afghanistan Call for Expression of Interest with a total budget of EUR 40 million and a submission deadline of 5 June 2026, focused on addressing migration drivers through economic stabilisation and community resilience. Under the exhaustive-list rule, ILO eligibility cannot be confirmed. Additionally, while the funding objective broadly aligns with ILO-implementable activities, eligibility cannot be established without confirming the entity-type list.

The guidelines PDF was not retrievable to confirm whether UN agencies/international organisations/IGOs are included. Without explicit inclusion of ILO-type entities, eligibility cannot be confirmed.

organization_type: Organisations meeting eligibility criteria (type not specified in visible content)

Online information session at 10:00 Brussels time
Submission deadline for concept notes at 17:00 Brussels time

eligible_activities: Economic stabilisation activities

Addressing drivers of migration and forced displacement

results.proposal_summary: The EU Delegation to Afghanistan (Reference: AFG/2026/Call for expression of interest) is issuing a Call for Expression of Interest with a total budget of EUR 40 million to address the drivers of migration and forced displacement by supporting economic stabilisation and strengthening community resilience in Afghanistan. The call is part of the EU's ongoing support for basic needs and livelihoods in Afghanistan. Eligible organisations are invited to submit concept notes by June 5, 2026 at 17:00 Brussels time. The eligible entity types are specified in the downloadable guidelines, which were not retrievable, so whether UN agencies or international organisations such as the ILO are eligible cannot be confirmed from the visible content. An information session was held on May 12, 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call targets economic stabilisation, livelihoods, and community resilience in a crisis context (Afghanistan), which aligns with ILO's mandate on decent work in crisis situations, employment policy, and labour migration, though ILO eligibility as an applicant entity type cannot be confirmed.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call targets Filipino MSMEs and networks of actors supporting circular economy transformation of private enterprises — not UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations. Additionally, the deadline (30 April 2026) has passed as of today (2026-05-25). The funding objective is primarily private-sector/MSME development, not ILO-implementable technical assistance.
results.classification_summary: This call targets Filipino MSMEs and networks supporting circular economy transformation in priority industries, with eligibility implicitly restricted to private-sector and business support entities — not UN agencies or IGOs. The extended deadline of 30 April 2026 has already passed. The funding objective centres on mobilising green business transformation among MSMEs, which is a private-sector development purpose outside ILO's implementing mandate.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call is framed exclusively for Filipino MSMEs and networks of actors in circular economy sectors (agriculture, fisheries, tourism, food, textile). The eligible entity types are implicitly private-sector/business networks and MSME support organisations; UN agencies, IGOs, or international organisations are not mentioned as eligible applicants.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call explicitly provides 1.8 million euros in 'financial assistance' described as a 'Transformation Grant' — a non-reimbursable grant instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Total grant pool is 1.8 million euros (PHP 125 million), well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The extended deadline is 30 April 2026. Today is 2026-05-25, so the deadline has passed.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The deadline of 30 April 2026 has passed; the call is no longer open for applications.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The primary objective is to accelerate the green/circular transformation of Filipino MSMEs — private-sector enterprise development. The grant funds business support programmes for private enterprises, not ILO-type technical assistance, labour standards, or social protection work.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is a grant facility for MSME transformation projects, not a procurement/tender for goods or services.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page does not explicitly state whether co-financing is mandatory.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call explicitly targets Filipino MSMEs operating in the Philippines, restricting eligibility to entities based in the Philippines.

results.title: Green Up to Scale Up: MSME Transformation Grant
results.deadline_text: deadline extended to 30 April
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies

In-person information session in Cebu (reserved slots)

eligible_activities: Establishment and implementation of business support programmes enabling circular transformation of MSMEs
Network-rooted solutions addressing broken links in circular economy development
Support in priority industries: agriculture and fisheries, tourism and hospitality, food, and textile
Activities driving economic and climate resilience
Minimizing organic, plastic and textile waste
funding_restrictions: Limited to activities in the Philippines
Focus restricted to priority industries: agriculture and fisheries, tourism and hospitality, food, textile
results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through its EU-Philippine Green Economy Partnership (EUR 60 million total programme), is funding the 'Green Up to Scale Up: MSME Transformation Grant' with EUR 1.8 million (PHP 125 million) in financial assistance and technical support. Managed by Expertise France and co-implemented with GGGI Philippines under the leadership of the Department of Trade and Industry (DTI), this call targets networks of actors in the Philippines supporting circular transformation of MSMEs in agriculture/fisheries, tourism/hospitality, food, and textile sectors. Eligible applicants are implicitly private-sector entities and MSME business support networks based in the Philippines. The extended submission deadline was 30 April 2026, which has now passed. The call does not identify UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations as eligible applicants.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on green/circular transformation of private MSMEs in the Philippines, with no alignment to ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or social dialogue — only a tangential connection through potential job creation in green sectors.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call is explicitly titled 'Civil Society' and targets civil society organisations, not UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations.

No mention of UN agencies, international organisations, or IGOs as eligible applicants.

results.proposal_summary: The FAIR Media Ukraine project, implemented by Internews International and funded by the European Union, invites Ukrainian media outlets from eleven frontline regions (Donetsk, Luhansk, Kharkiv, Sumy, Chernihiv, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson, Mykolaiv, Odesa, Dnipropetrovsk, and Kyiv) to apply for grants averaging approximately EUR 15,000 each to support projects lasting up to eight months. The competition aims to strengthen regional and local Ukrainian media operating under wartime conditions, helping them deliver trusted, high-quality information to war-affected communities and build medium-term organisational resilience. Eligible applicants must be registered in Ukraine, demonstrate editorial independence, maintain an operational presence in the regions they serve, and must not have previously received a FAIR Media Ukraine grant. The proposal deadline was 11 May 2026 at 18:00 Kyiv time.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on media development and information access for war-affected communities in Ukraine, which is outside the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work.

results.title: Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) — African Development Bank
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
results.proposal_summary: The Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) is an African Development Bank initiative designed to mobilise public and private finance for climate change adaptation by certifying adaptation benefits of projects and facilitating payments to project developers. The pilot phase ran from 2019 to 2023 with 10–12 demonstration projects across Africa; as of November 2025, the pilot phase has concluded and the AfDB is moving to establish a permanent secretariat by 2027. The page is a programme information page — no open Call for Proposals, submission deadline, grant amounts, or eligible applicant categories are currently specified. The ILO is not eligible under this mechanism.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the ABM mentions job creation for vulnerable groups and women as one adaptation benefit concept, its core purpose is a financial certification and investment mobilisation mechanism — not labour standards, employment policy, or social protection work within the ILO's mandate.

results.title: ADF Climate Action Window
results.organization: African Development Fund (African Development Bank Group)
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Adaptation action in agriculture and food security, water security, climate information and early warning, green transport and infrastructure, green energy and energy efficiency, green finance

results.proposal_summary: The ADF Climate Action Window is a dedicated financing window established by the African Development Fund (ADF) under its 16th replenishment cycle to help address Africa's climate financing gap of approximately $60 billion annually. Resources are allocated through three sub-windows: 75% for adaptation, 15% for mitigation, and 10% for technical assistance, covering six thematic sectors including agriculture, water security, green energy, and green finance across ADF-eligible low-income African countries. The window offers a large grant component to minimise debt distress. This page is a general programme description landing page; no specific open call deadline or application window is currently stated on the page.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The window focuses primarily on climate finance mobilisation and investment-ready projects in Africa, with only a minor technical assistance component; this framing is peripheral to ILO's core mandate of labour standards, decent work, and social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Adaptation Acceleration Program (AAAP) is a joint initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) and the Global Center on Adaptation (GCA) launched in 2021, aiming to mobilise $25 billion over five years to accelerate climate adaptation across Africa. The programme operates through four pillars: climate-smart digital agriculture, infrastructure resilience, youth empowerment for green jobs, and innovative adaptation finance. It is implemented via an Upstream Financing Facility at GCA and the ADF Climate Action Window (up to $13 billion). The programme targets all African countries, with a particular focus on sub-Saharan Africa's most climate-vulnerable low-income nations. This page is a general programme description, not a specific open Call for Proposals; no application deadline, funding round, or eligibility criteria for external applicants are specified.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the AAAP includes a youth employment and green jobs pillar that partially aligns with ILO's mandate on skills development and employment policy, the programme's primary focus is climate adaptation finance mobilisation and infrastructure investment, which is peripheral to ILO's core decent work and labour standards mandate.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Climate Risk Insurance Framework for Adaptation (ACRIFA) is an African Development Bank initiative aiming to raise $1 billion in concessional capital and grants to develop and scale climate risk insurance for Africa's agricultural sector. It operates through two windows: an investment facility (concessional debt, equity, guarantees) and a technical assistance facility (grants) targeting insurance providers, reinsurers, regulators, and agri-SMEs across African countries. The initiative is not structured as an open call for proposals and does not specify submission deadlines or eligibility for UN/IGO applicants. results.title: Africa Disaster Risk Financing Programme (ADRiFi)
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Climate disaster risk finance and insurance (CDRFI) capacity development
Premium support/subsidies for participation in regional climate risk pools
Climate risk data collection and dissemination
Contingency and response planning
Disaster risk financing diagnostics and strategies
results.proposal_summary: The Africa Disaster Risk Financing Programme (ADRiFi), initiated by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in 2018 and supported through a Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF) established in 2021, aims to enhance the resilience of African Regional Member Countries to climate shocks by improving disaster risk management and financing through climate insurance solutions. The MDTF has received approximately USD 97 million from Canada, Switzerland, Norway, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, and the United States (USAID and State Department). Eligible beneficiaries are African Regional Member Countries, which access support via formal requests to the Bank for premium subsidies to participate in the African Risk Capacity regional risk pool and for capacity development. The page is a general programme description with no open call for proposals, no submission deadline, and no active application window.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the programme addresses climate resilience and social protection for vulnerable populations—topics tangentially related to ILO mandates on decent work and crisis response—it is primarily a sovereign climate insurance and disaster risk financing mechanism for African governments, with no direct alignment to ILO's core labour standards, employment, or social dialogue mandate.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Fertilizer Financing Mechanism (AFFM) is a special fund administered by the African Development Bank Group, established by African Union member states to provide innovative financing solutions for fertilizer value chains in Africa. The mechanism operates through trade credit guarantees and grant instruments targeting smallholder farmers, agro-dealers, and financial intermediaries across African countries including Kenya, Uganda, Mozambique, Tanzania, Zimbabwe, Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Nigeria. The page is a general programme description page and does not constitute an active Call for Proposals; no application window, deadline, or defined applicant eligibility criteria for a specific funding round are present.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AFFM focuses on agricultural financing and fertilizer value chains, which falls outside the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) is inviting proposals under the MENTARI 2 programme, a bilateral UK-Indonesia partnership supporting Indonesia's energy transition and implementation of the UK-Indonesia Strategic Partnership in the energy sector. The call funds activities in the areas of energy policy reform, investment support for small-scale renewable energy projects, and mainstreaming of gender equality, disability, and social inclusion (GEDSI). The grant is valued at more than £1,000,000 and will support implementation from July 2026 to March 2028. Eligible applicants are legally constituted non-profit organisations including NGOs, research institutions, universities, foundations, and UK-based non-profits; UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations are not eligible. The application deadline is 19 June 2026, and proposals must be submitted via email to mentari2@fcdo.gov.uk. No co-financing requirement is stated.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on renewable energy policy reform and investment support in Indonesia, which does not align with the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, decent work, social protection, or employment policy, though GEDSI mainstreaming is a minor component.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) has launched the Humanitarian Emergency and Longer-term Planning – Sudan (HELP-S) programme, a £30 million, 2.5-year grant (2026–2029) aimed at strengthening local responders and community resilience in the more stable, conflict-affected areas of Sudan. The programme prioritises locally-led humanitarian approaches, genuine partnership with Sudanese organisations, and filling response gaps in high-need areas across health, livelihoods, and gender. Eligible applicants include international NGOs, Sudanese civil society organisations, UN agencies (including the ILO), not-for-profit academic institutions, and mixed consortia (with for-profit entities only as downstream partners). Applicants must demonstrate proven humanitarian delivery experience, capacity to operate in hard-to-reach areas, strong safeguarding systems, and robust financial management. The first mandatory submission step is an Expression of Interest (5-page template plus high-level budget) to be emailed to Daniel.diriba@fcdo.gov.uk by 11:59pm EAT on 27 May 2026. No co-financing requirement is stated.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call targets humanitarian response, livelihoods, and community resilience in conflict-affected Sudan, which aligns with ILO's mandate on decent work in crisis and post-crisis situations and employment/livelihoods programming, though it is primarily a humanitarian emergency response programme rather than a labour standards or social protection intervention.

results.proposal_summary: The UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO) is inviting proposals for the protection component of the Jordan Humanitarian and Systems Strengthening Programme (JHASS). The programme will provide up to £7 million through an accountable grant agreement to a single NGO-led consortium to deliver protection services — including GBV prevention and response, child protection, psychosocial support, and community-based protection — for refugees and vulnerable Jordanians in non-camp settings. The programme is expected to run from September 2026 to March 2030, subject to annual funding confirmation. Eligible lead organisations must be non-profit INGOs legally registered in Jordan and their country of incorporation, with relevant humanitarian protection experience; national and local NGOs/CBOs are strongly encouraged as consortium partners. Commercial and for-profit organisations are not eligible as lead organisations. Full proposals must be submitted by 28 June 2026, with clarification questions due by 1 June 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call addresses child labour, GBV, social inclusion, and protection of vulnerable workers/refugees, which partially overlaps with ILO themes on fundamental rights at work and decent work in crisis settings, but the primary focus is humanitarian protection programming led by INGOs rather than labour standards or employment policy work.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – Jamaica 2026 is a small-grant programme administered by the High Commission of Canada in Jamaica under Global Affairs Canada. It supports small-scale, high-impact local development projects aligned with Canadian thematic priorities. The programme is primarily directed at local civil society organisations, NGOs, academic institutions, and intergovernmental/multilateral organisations working with local partners in Jamaica. Average contributions range from CAD 15,000 to CAD 25,000. No co-financing requirement or specific accreditation is mentioned. Applications must be submitted using the designated form in English or French; only successful applicants are contacted. The specific submission deadline is not provided in the available text.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CFLI supports local development projects in Jamaica broadly aligned with development goals, but its primary focus is not specifically on labour standards, decent work, or ILO mandate areas, and the grant size is far below the USD 50,000 threshold.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call targets local actors and civil society organizations (projects 'conceived and designed predominantly by local actors'), not UN agencies or intergovernmental organizations. The maximum grant is CAD 100,000, which at current exchange rates is below USD 50,000 only if interpreted strictly, but the primary exclusion is entity-type ineligibility. Additionally, the average grant is CAD 30,000–50,000, well below the USD 50,000 threshold.
results.classification_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – Ukraine 2026 is directed at local actors and civil society organizations in Ukraine; UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations such as the ILO are not among the eligible applicant categories. The maximum grant of CAD 100,000 (approximately USD 73,000) technically exceeds the USD 50,000 threshold, but the average grant of CAD 30,000–50,000 is well below it, and the program is explicitly designed for locally conceived, small-scale projects by local actors. The ILO fails the eligibility test on entity type grounds as the call does not name UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, or equivalent multilateral entities as eligible applicants.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CFLI is 'directed at projects conceived and designed predominantly by local actors' and supports 'local endeavours' and 'civil societies.' The eligible applicant category is local/civil society organizations; UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, or multilateral entities are not listed as eligible.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The program provides non-reimbursable 'contributions' (contribution agreements), which are grants. No repayment obligation is indicated.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The average CFLI contribution is CAD 30,000–50,000, with a maximum of CAD 100,000. The average grant range is below the USD 50,000 threshold; even the maximum of CAD 100,000 is only approximately USD 73,000, but the program's stated average is CAD 30,000–50,000 (roughly USD 22,000–37,000), which is below threshold. The maximum could pass, but typical/average awards do not.

Deadline is future.evidence: The submission deadline is June 10, 2026 at 23:59 GMT+3, which is in the future relative to today's date of May 25, 2026.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: This is a single specific call for proposals for the 2026 CFLI cycle in Ukraine, with a clear submission deadline of June 10, 2026, an application form, and submission instructions.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The CFLI supports small-scale, high-impact projects aligned with Global Affairs Canada's thematic priorities (likely including governance, human rights, gender equality, democracy), implemented through technical assistance and community-level activities — the type of programmatic work the ILO could implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for proposals for project grants, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page does not explicitly mention co-financing requirements; the Annex with full eligibility details is not fully visible in the provided content.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the visible text.

It supports small-scale, high-impact projects aligned with Canada's thematic development priorities, conceived and designed predominantly by local actors in Ukraine. Grants average CAD 30,000–50,000 with a maximum of CAD 100,000, disbursed as non-reimbursable contributions converted to Ukrainian hryvnia. results.ilo_match_evidence: While the CFLI may support activities touching on social issues in crisis-affected Ukraine, the program is explicitly designed for small-scale local civil society projects and does not align with the ILO's mandate as an intergovernmental technical assistance organization implementing labour standards, employment, and social protection programmes.

results.proposal_summary: Norway (via Norad) has allocated 100 million NOK for private sector development in Ukraine under the Nansen Support Programme (budget item 159.73) for 2026–2027. The call aims to promote investment-ready commercial projects, mobilise private capital, create jobs, and improve framework conditions for the private sector in Ukraine, with priority given to sustainable energy, food security/agriculture, and critical infrastructure reconstruction. The call is open to multilateral organisations, Norwegian and foreign non-profit and commercial organisations, and state organisations from any country. Applications must be submitted via the Norad Grants Portal by 17 June 2026. Co-financing is encouraged but not explicitly mandatory, and indirect costs are capped at 7% of project operating costs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call mentions job creation and improving private sector framework conditions — themes tangentially related to ILO's decent work mandate — the primary objective is mobilising private capital and funding investment-ready commercial projects, which falls outside ILO's core technical assistance and policy implementation mandate.

Conditionally repayable contributions to sustainable development projects
Mobilizing private finance for international assistance projects
results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada's Innovative Finance Programs page is a landing/aggregator page describing three 5-year pilot programs: the International Assistance Innovation Program, the Sovereign Loans Program, and Conditionally Repayable Contributions. These programs aim to mobilize private capital, provide sovereign loans, and introduce conditionally repayable contribution tools to support sustainable development goals in least-developed and low-income countries. The page does not constitute a single open call for proposals and provides no submission deadline, eligible applicant categories, or funding thresholds. As a general program description page with no active application window, it is not a CfP to which the ILO could apply.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The programs are focused on innovative/blended finance mechanisms (loans, conditionally repayable contributions, private capital mobilization) rather than the technical assistance, labour standards, or social protection work that constitutes the ILO's core mandate.

results.exclusion_reason: This is an aggregator/index page listing 121 individual country-level calls, not a single specific open call. Additionally, eligible applicants are explicitly described as 'local organizations', which excludes the ILO as a UN intergovernmental organization. Furthermore, the average grant size is stated as approximately $31,000 CAD, which is below the USD 50,000 threshold.
results.classification_summary: The CFLI index page is an aggregator listing 121 country-level calls rather than a single specific open call, failing the 'Is open call with active application window' criterion. Eligible applicants are explicitly 'local organizations', which excludes the ILO as a UN intergovernmental organization. The average project size of CAD 31,000 is below the USD 50,000 minimum threshold. These three independent grounds render the opportunity ineligible.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page states 'Local organizations plan and carry out projects' — the eligible applicant category is explicitly 'local organizations', which does not include UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, or multilateral institutions. ILO is not a local organization.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CFLI provides non-reimbursable grant funding (Official Development Assistance) to local organizations for small projects — no repayment obligation indicated.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The page states 'projects averaging $31,000 each' (CAD), which is well below the USD 50,000 threshold even accounting for currency conversion.

Deadline is future.evidence: Several calls listed have deadlines in June 2026 (e.g., Jamaica 2026-06-12, Ukraine 2026-06-10), which are after today (2026-05-25). However, some listed deadlines (Serbia, Montenegro, North Macedonia: 2026-05-08; Bosnia and Herzegovina, Benin: 2026-05-22; Albania: 2026-05-21; Armenia: 2026-05-15) are already past.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: This is an aggregator/index page listing 121 individual country-level calls with separate deadlines, eligibility criteria, and application processes. It does not describe a single specific call with one set of eligibility rules and one deadline.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The CFLI funds small, high-impact local projects in ODA-eligible countries across various development themes. Without reviewing individual country calls, the specific thematic focus cannot be confirmed as aligned with ILO mandate, though some could be.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The page describes grant calls for proposals, not procurement of goods/services or consultancy tenders.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing requirement is mentioned on the index page; individual country calls may specify requirements.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are specified on the index page.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Each call is country-specific and targets 'local organizations' in the respective eligible country, effectively restricting applicants to locally registered entities in each target country.

results.title: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI)
results.deadline_text: 2026-06-12 at 23:59 (GMT+5) [Jamaica - earliest future deadline shown]

application_process.description: Application submission deadline (Jamaica - latest open deadline shown on index page)

eligible_activities: Small, high-impact local development projects in ODA-eligible countries
Projects selected and approved by Canadian Embassy or High Commission
funding_restrictions: Projects must be in ODA-eligible countries
Only local organizations are eligible to apply
Average project size approximately CAD 31,000
results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI), administered by the Government of Canada (Global Affairs Canada), provides small grants averaging CAD 31,000 to local organizations for high-impact development projects in over 120 ODA-eligible countries. The program operates through individual country-level calls managed by Canadian Embassies and High Commissions. Only local organizations are eligible to apply; international organizations including UN agencies are not listed as eligible applicants. The index page is an aggregator of 121 country-specific calls with varying deadlines, the latest open deadline shown being June 12, 2026 for Jamaica. The ILO is ineligible due to its status as an intergovernmental organization rather than a local entity, and due to the average grant size being below the USD 50,000 threshold.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some CFLI country calls may touch on decent work or social development themes, the program is explicitly designed for local organizations implementing small community projects and does not align with ILO's mandate as an implementing partner for technical assistance and policy work at scale.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline has passed (April 16, 2025) and the call is explicitly closed. Additionally, the ILO as a UN intergovernmental organization is not among the eligible applicant types named in this call.
results.classification_summary: This call for concept notes from Global Affairs Canada (GAC) for health, education, and women's and girls' rights in Afghanistan is explicitly closed, with the submission deadline of April 16, 2025 having passed. The page states 'This funding opportunity process is now closed.' Furthermore, the eligible applicant categories named do not include UN agencies or intergovernmental organizations, and a mandatory 5% cost-sharing requirement applies. The ILO is ineligible on both the closed deadline and entity-type grounds.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call targets organizations that can register on the Partners@International portal; eligible types referenced include civil society and consortium partners, with no mention of UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, or multilateral institutions as eligible applicants.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call provides non-repayable contributions/grants (GAC's Policy on Cost-Sharing for Grant and Non-Repayable Contributions is referenced), with no loan or equity component.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: GAC anticipates supporting 4 to 8 projects of CAD$2,000,000 to CAD$10,000,000 each, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The page explicitly states 'This funding opportunity process is now closed' and the deadline was April 16, 2025, which is before today's date of May 25, 2026.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page explicitly states 'This funding opportunity process is now closed' with a past deadline of April 16, 2025.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call funds projects supporting delivery of basic needs services (health, education, women's and girls' rights and empowerment) to Afghans, which aligns with technical assistance and programmatic activities ILO could implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is a Call for Concept Notes for project grants, not a procurement tender or RFP for goods/services.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The supporting document explicitly states: 'Your organization will provide at least 5% of the total eligible direct project costs over the life of the project in cash and/or in-kind,' making cost-sharing a mandatory requirement.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required; applicants must register on the Partners@International portal.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call mentions consortium applications and registration of co-signing organizations, but the truncated content does not explicitly state whether a non-UN entity must lead the consortium.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is issued by Global Affairs Canada but the full eligibility text regarding applicant registration country is not fully visible in the truncated content.

results.title: Call for Concept Notes – Health, Education and Women's and Girls' Rights and Empowerment in Afghanistan
results.deadline_text: Submit your application by or before 12 pm (noon) EST April 16, 2025.
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Stage 1: Submission of concept note via Partners@International portal by 12 pm (noon) EST
Stage 2: Full proposal submission (by invitation only, following concept note review)

eligible_activities: Delivery of basic needs services to Afghans, especially women and girls
Health programming
Education programming
Women's and girls' rights and empowerment programming
funding_restrictions: Funding cannot be provided based on a concept note alone
Late submissions will not be accepted
Minimum 5% cost-sharing of total eligible direct project costs required (cash and/or in-kind)
results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada (GAC) issued a two-stage Call for Concept Notes for projects supporting health, education, and women's and girls' rights and empowerment in Afghanistan. The total available funding is up to CAD$35,000,000 over 4 years, with 4 to 8 projects expected at CAD$2,000,000 to CAD$10,000,000 each. Projects must address the delivery of basic needs services to Afghans, especially women and girls, across one or more of three programming areas (health, education, women's/girls' rights and empowerment). Eligible applicants are organizations able to register on the Partners@International portal; UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not listed as eligible entity types. A mandatory minimum 5% cost-share of total eligible direct project costs is required. The concept note deadline was April 16, 2025, and the call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call addresses women's rights, health, and education in a crisis context (Afghanistan), which are adjacent to ILO's decent work and social protection mandate, the ILO is not an eligible applicant and the call is closed.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call explicitly restricts applicants to Swedish universities/research organisations and international universities or research organizations with strong links to Sweden and LMICs or Ukraine. UN agencies, IGOs, and intergovernmental organisations are not listed among eligible applicant types.
results.classification_summary: This Sida call for concept notes targets Swedish and international universities and research organisations with strong links to Sweden and LMICs or Ukraine. The eligible applicant list is exhaustive and limited to universities and research organisations; UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations such as the ILO are not included. The call is a genuine, currently open single call with a deadline of 16 August 2026, and the grant size (SEK 8–14 million) exceeds the USD 50,000 threshold, but ILO ineligibility is a hard exclusion.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly states eligibility is limited to 'Swedish universities/research organisations with strong links to LMICs or Ukraine' and 'organisations/universities based in other countries' provided they partner with a Swedish university or research organization. The exhaustive eligible categories are universities and research organisations; UN agencies, IGOs, and intergovernmental organisations are not mentioned.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: Sida is offering non-reimbursable development cooperation funding (grant/contribution) to support research cooperation partnerships, with no mention of loans, equity, or repayment obligations.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The call requests Sida-funding for a total amount between SEK 8–14 million per initiative, which substantially exceeds USD 50,000.

Deadline is future.evidence: Concept notes should be submitted no later than 16 August 2026 at 17:00 CEST, which is after today's date of 2026-05-25.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The call opened on 25 May 2026 at 09:00 CEST with a submission deadline of 16 August 2026 at 17:00 CEST. It describes a single specific call with an explicit application window, a portal link, and a named funding cycle.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The primary purpose is to fund research cooperation partnerships between Swedish and LMIC/Ukraine research organisations. The call is framed as research capacity building and impact enhancement, which is implementable in principle, but the applicant type restriction to universities/research organisations makes it moot. The objective itself (health/SRHR research cooperation) is not within ILO's core mandate areas.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant call for concept notes, not a procurement/tender for goods or services.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Co-financing from other sources is welcomed but explicitly not required: 'The call welcomes (but does not require): Initiatives that are co-financed from other sources.'

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. The call requires the agreement partner to be a legal entity capable of entering into an agreement with Sida and to meet Sida's transparency/accountability requirements, but this is not a formal accreditation with an external body.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are specified beyond requiring at least one Swedish university or research organization and at least one LMIC/Ukraine research organization. No rule mandates that a non-UN entity must lead.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Swedish universities/research organisations are the primary eligible applicants. International organisations may apply only if they partner with at least one Swedish university or research organization and have strong links to Sweden and LMICs or Ukraine, effectively tying eligibility to a Swedish connection.

results.title: Invitation for concept notes for health/SRHR development research cooperation in LMICs or Ukraine
results.deadline_text: 16 August 2026 at 17:00 CEST
results.organization: Sweden (Sida – Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency)

application_process.description: Step 1: Submission of concept notes (deadline 16 August 2026 at 17:00 CEST)
Step 2: Invitation to submit full proposals (preliminary deadline 31 October 2026)

eligible_activities: Strengthening research capacity in health and/or SRHR in LMICs or Ukraine
Facilitating increased impact of scientific knowledge for improved health and/or SRHR in LMICs or Ukraine
Equitable research cooperation partnerships including co-production of proposals
Joint definition of research and cooperation agendas
Fair distribution of roles, responsibilities, and resources between Swedish and LMIC/Ukraine partners
Gender equality integration in research and development activities
Research activities embedding knowledge production in local contexts
funding_restrictions: No support solely or primarily for individual Masters or PhD degrees
No support solely or primarily for networking activities or conferences
No support solely or primarily for design or delivery of Masters or PhD programmes or courses
Concept notes must not exceed five pages (11pt font, no additional annexes)
results.proposal_summary: Sida (Sweden) invites concept notes for health/SRHR development research cooperation in low and lower-middle income countries (LMICs) or Ukraine. The call seeks to support research cooperation partnerships between Swedish organisations and organisations in LMICs or Ukraine, contributing to research, strengthened research capacity, and research impact in human health and/or sexual and reproductive health and rights. Eligible applicants are Swedish universities/research organisations with strong links to LMICs or Ukraine, or international universities/research organisations partnering with at least one Swedish research organisation. Grant amounts range from SEK 8–14 million per initiative, with implementation periods of 18–24 months feasible between January 2027 and December 2028. The call opened on 25 May 2026 with a concept note deadline of 16 August 2026; selected applicants will be invited to submit full proposals by 31 October 2026. Co-financing is welcomed but not required.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses on health and SRHR research cooperation, which falls outside the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work, and the eligible applicant categories (universities and research organisations) explicitly exclude UN agencies such as the ILO.

results.exclusion_reason: This page is not an open call for proposals; it is a procedural update/announcement describing changes to the operationalisation of an existing CSO strategy. It does not constitute a currently-open call with a submission deadline. Additionally, eligibility is explicitly restricted to civil society organisations (CSOs), excluding the ILO as a UN intergovernmental organisation.
results.classification_summary: The page is an informational update to applicant organisations under Sida's CSO Strategy 2025–2029, not an open call for proposals with an active application window or submission deadline. Eligible applicants are civil society organisations, explicitly excluding party-affiliated organisations and requiring 15% own funds from non-public sources; UN/IGO entities such as the ILO are not named as eligible. The next call for proposals is planned for 2027. Both the ILO eligibility and open-call criteria fail.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The strategy exclusively targets civil society organisations (CSOs). The text explicitly discusses eligibility of 'civil society organisations based in countries other than developing countries' and party-affiliated organisations (PAOs). UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or international organisations are not mentioned as eligible entity types. The ILO does not fall within the CSO category.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page references Sida agreements and 'support' under the CSO strategy, which are non-reimbursable grant-type contributions typical of Sida's development cooperation funding.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific grant amounts are stated on the page; only 'indicative amounts' are referenced without figures. Cannot confirm whether amounts exceed USD 50,000.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline is provided. The page is an update about decisions already taken in June 2025. The next call for proposals is planned for 2027 but no current open deadline exists.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an informational update about decisions made regarding the first call under the CSO Strategy 2025–2029, not an active call for proposals. The next call is planned for 2027. There is no application mechanism, submission deadline, or open window described.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call is directed at civil society organisations for their programmatic work; the ILO is not a CSO and cannot apply under this strategy. The broader framing targets CSO capacity and civil society development activities, not ILO-type technical assistance mandates.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The page concerns grant support to CSOs, not procurement of goods/services or consultancy tenders.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The strategy requires organisations to contribute 15% own funds (defined as donations, sponsorship, membership fees, lottery proceeds, revenue from sale of goods/services) for activities carried out by civil society organisations based outside developing countries.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the page text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The strategy appears open to CSOs from various countries (both Swedish-based and others), with different own-fund rules depending on whether the organisation is based in a developing country or not. No restriction to Swedish-registered organisations only is stated.

results.proposal_summary: This page is an informational update from Sida (Sweden's development cooperation agency) addressed to organisations that applied under the CSO Strategy 2025–2029. It announces three key decisions: (1) party-affiliated organisations are excluded from new agreements under this strategy; (2) the definition of own funds (15% co-financing requirement for non-developing-country CSOs) now excludes public resources; and (3) Sida can sign three-year agreements with extension options. The first call has already been processed, with indicative amounts communicated to applicants in June 2025. A new call for proposals for major grants is planned for 2027, with new agreements from 2028. Eligibility is restricted to civil society organisations; UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations such as the ILO are not eligible. No funding amounts or submission deadline are stated on this page.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CSO strategy focuses on civil society strengthening and development cooperation broadly, with no specific alignment to ILO's mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work, and the ILO is ineligible as a UN IGO.

results.exclusion_reason: The call is restricted to universities and research institutions in Denmark. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organisation, is not a Danish university or research institution and is explicitly excluded by the applicant category restriction. Additionally, Phase 1 of this call is explicitly marked as 'Closed – for information only', meaning the application window has passed.
results.classification_summary: This Danida Fellowship Centre call is restricted to 'universities and research institutions in Denmark,' which is an exhaustive list of eligible applicant types that does not include UN agencies, international organisations, or intergovernmental organisations; the ILO is therefore ineligible. Phase 1 is explicitly marked 'Closed – for information only,' confirming the application window has passed. The call also restricts applicants by domestic registration in Denmark, further excluding the ILO.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly states 'Universities and research institutions in Denmark can apply for funds.' This exhaustive category does not include UN agencies, international organisations, or intergovernmental organisations. The ILO is none of these eligible entity types.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call provides grants (non-reimbursable funds) of up to 10 million DKK for research collaboration projects, with no mention of repayment obligations.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Grants are up to 10 million DKK (approximately USD 1.4 million), well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The page explicitly states 'Closed – for information only' for Phase 1 of the 2026 Call for Applications, indicating the deadline has already passed.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page explicitly states the call is 'Closed – for information only,' confirming this is a finished call and no active application window exists.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call funds collaborative research projects with development cooperation themes, which is the type of programmatic activity an ILO team could implement, though eligibility is restricted to Danish universities.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a research grant call, not a procurement tender or consultancy RFP.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing requirement is mentioned in the call text.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call mentions North-South partnerships and team composition but does not explicitly state consortium leadership rules regarding who must lead.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call explicitly states that 'Universities and research institutions in Denmark can apply,' restricting eligibility to Danish institutions only.

results.title: Danida Call for Applications 2026 – Phase 1 (Research Funding)
results.deadline_text: Closed – for information only
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

Selected projects can start up in April 2027

eligible_activities: Collaborative research projects on development cooperation themes
Capacity strengthening activities beyond PhD training

Knowledge production and dissemination

results.proposal_summary: The Danida Fellowship Centre, on behalf of the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Danida), issued an annual Call for Applications 2026 (Phase 1) for research collaboration projects in development cooperation. Grants of up to 10 million DKK per project, with a duration of up to five years, are available exclusively to universities and research institutions in Denmark. The thematic framework is defined by Denmark's development cooperation strategies 'A Changing World – Partnerships in Development' and 'Africa's Century,' with a focus on developing and growth countries prioritised by Danida. Phase 1 is now closed, with prequalified applicants invited to submit full project proposals in Phase 2; selected projects can start in April 2027. The ILO is ineligible as the call is restricted to Danish academic and research institutions.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call funds development research that could touch on labour, employment, and social issues, it is exclusively open to Danish universities and research institutions, making ILO participation structurally impossible, and its primary focus is academic research collaboration rather than ILO's core mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call targets research institutions/universities and their partners (Principal Investigators, academic teams, responsible institutions), not UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations. The eligible applicant category is implicitly restricted to research/academic institutions.
results.classification_summary: This Danida Fellowship Centre call targets collaborative research projects led by universities and research institutions (responsible institutions with Principal Investigators), not UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations. The ILO is therefore ineligible. The call is a genuine research grant with a maximum of 10,000,000 DKK, running from April 2026 to no later than March 2031, and is currently open (Phase 2 application system is live). However, ILO eligibility fails because the applicant category is exclusively academic/research institutions.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call is structured around 'responsible institutions' led by a 'Principal Investigator' with academic credentials, with budget forms for universities and research partners. No mention of UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or IGOs as eligible applicants. The framing is entirely academic/research-institution-oriented.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call provides non-reimbursable research grants (budget template shows grant funding with no repayment obligation). The maximum total budget is stated as 10,000,000 DKK.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Maximum grant is 10,000,000 DKK, far above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The page does not explicitly state a submission deadline date for Phase 2. The application system is live and guidelines reference 2025 Phase 2, but no specific deadline date is visible in the extracted content.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: No concrete submission deadline or dated application milestone is present; page appears to describe a process or programme rather than an active call.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call funds genuine academic research projects requiring a Principal Investigator and institutional research partnerships between Danish and developing-country universities. This is not programmatic technical assistance or policy work of the type ILO implements.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a research grant call, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is mentioned in the available content.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call requires a 'responsible institution' to lead, which in context means a Danish or partner-country research/academic institution, but no explicit rule excluding UN leadership is stated.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is administered by Danida Fellowship Centre and focuses on Danida partner countries for research implementation, with a Danish institution typically serving as the responsible institution. The geographic focus is on Denmark and Danida priority partner countries.

results.proposal_summary: The Danida Fellowship Centre, on behalf of Denmark's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Danida), is running Call 2025 – Research Grants, Phase 2, which provides non-reimbursable research grants of up to DKK 10,000,000 to collaborative research partnerships between Danish and developing-country academic or research institutions. Projects must start in April 2026 and conclude no later than March 2031. Eligible applicants are research institutions led by a qualified Principal Investigator, with equitable North-South partnerships. The call focuses on development research aligned with Danida priority countries and the SDGs. Applications are submitted through an online portal (smapply). No explicit submission deadline was identified in the available content. The ILO is not an eligible applicant as the call targets academic/research institutions.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some funded research could touch on decent work or social development themes, the call is fundamentally an academic research grant requiring institutional PI-led research partnerships, which falls outside ILO's mandate and organizational profile as an IGO implementing technical assistance and policy work.

results.proposal_summary: The Food Exhibition Explorer is a project grant administered by Business Finland, Finland's innovation funding agency, to support Finnish SMEs and midcap food and beverage companies in participating in international B2B trade fairs outside Finland. The primary focus is sustainable tourism development and territorial heritage conservation in the transboundary Dinaric Alps area. results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons (TIP Office) issued a Notice of Intent on December 3, 2024, signaling its intent to award one or more cooperative agreements under the Program to End Modern Slavery (PEMS) FY 2025. Projects must address human trafficking outside of the United States
results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons (TIP Office) runs an annual, full and open competition for grants to combat human trafficking outside the United States. Eligible applicants include U.S.-based and foreign non-profits, NGOs, for-profit organizations (without profit generation), higher education institutions, public international organizations, and U.S. government agencies; foreign governments are excluded. Projects must address one or more of the '3Ps' (prosecution, protection, prevention) related to sex and/or labor trafficking. Awards have historically ranged up to USD 1,000,000 over two to three years, though larger, longer-term proposals are welcomed. The process begins with a Notice of Funding Opportunity (NOFO) inviting Statements of Interest, followed by a second stage for full proposals from selected applicants. No specific current NOFO deadline or open application window is identified on this page, which describes the general annual process rather than a specific open call. Contact: JTIPGrants@state.gov.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The TIP Office grants directly address labor trafficking, forced labor, and fundamental principles and rights at work — core ILO mandate areas — and PIOs including UN specialized agencies are explicitly eligible, making this thematically well-aligned with ILO's decent work and anti-forced-labour mandate.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call is explicitly open only to national or international associations, NGOs, civil society organisations, and consortiums formed by non-profit structures and private law companies. UN agencies/IGOs are not listed among eligible applicants.
results.classification_summary: This AFD/EU-funded call targets associations, NGOs, civil society organisations, and non-profit consortiums to implement Phase 3 of the urban nursery programme (Pépinière urbaine) in Tunisia. The eligible applicant categories are exhaustive and do not include UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or international institutions. The ILO is therefore ineligible. Additionally, the call's focus on urban rehabilitation, participatory design of public spaces, and equipment management falls outside the ILO's core mandate areas.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call states: 'L'appel à initiatives est ouvert aux associations nationales ou internationales, ONG, organisations de la société civile, et aux consortiums formés par des structures à but non lucratif et des sociétés de droit privé.' This exhaustive list does not include UN agencies, IGOs, or international organisations.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The budget of 788,000 EUR is described as a 'subvention déléguée de l'Union Européenne' (delegated grant from the EU), which is a non-reimbursable contribution.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The available budget is 788,000 EUR, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The closing date is 04/06/2026 at 12:00 Paris time, which is after today's date of 2026-05-18.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The call opened on 21 April 2026 and closes on 4 June 2026. It describes a single specific call with a named submission deadline and an online application portal.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The primary purpose is urban rehabilitation, participatory design of public spaces and collective equipment, testing management models for urban facilities, and capacity building for ARRU. This is urban development/planning work, not ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is described as an 'appel à initiatives' (call for initiatives/proposals) for a project grant, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is mentioned in the call text.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call allows consortiums formed by non-profit structures and private law companies but does not specify leadership rules excluding UN entities in consortium arrangements.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is open to national or international associations, NGOs, and civil society organisations, without restriction to a specific country of registration.

results.proposal_summary: AFD, using delegated EU grant funds, is launching a call for initiatives to select the implementing operator ('pépiniériste') for Phase 3 of the Urban Nursery (Pépinière Urbaine) programme in Tunisia. The programme is part of the PROVILLE 2 rehabilitation programme and will be deployed in the cities of Menzel Abderrahman (Bizerte governorate) and Teboursouk (Béja governorate). The available budget is 788,000 EUR as a non-reimbursable grant, and the project must be implemented over a maximum of 24 months. Eligible applicants are national or international associations, NGOs, civil society organisations, and consortiums of non-profit and private law structures. The deadline for submission is 4 June 2026 at 12:00 Paris time. No co-financing requirement is mentioned. The objective is to improve programming and management of urban equipment, test participatory approaches, and strengthen the operational capacities of the ARRU.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses exclusively on urban rehabilitation, participatory design of public spaces, and management of collective urban equipment in Tunisia, with no connection to ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.

All calls currently visible on the page are closed, with the most recent deadline having passed in May 2025. No single open call with a future deadline or active application window is present. results.exclusion_reason: Aggregator/index page listing multiple past and current CSO financing mechanisms; no single open call with a specific deadline. Eligible applicants are explicitly CSOs/NGOs, not UN agencies or IGOs.
results.classification_summary: This page is an aggregator/landing page describing multiple AFD CSO financing mechanisms (CSO Initiatives Platform, FISONG, APCC, FSOF, small-scale programs), not a single specific open call for proposals. Additionally, all eligible applicant categories are explicitly limited to CSOs, NGOs, French associations, foundations, and non-profit organizations — UN agencies and intergovernmental organizations are not mentioned as eligible. No specific open application window with a future deadline is identified on the page.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page explicitly lists eligible applicants as French associations, syndicates, foundations under French law, local CSOs/NGOs, and international non-profit organizations. UN agencies, intergovernmental organizations, and IGOs are not included in the eligible applicant categories.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page describes co-financing grants (subventions) and non-reimbursable contributions for CSO projects.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The CSO Initiatives Platform requires a minimum total project amount of €300,000–€500,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: No specific open deadline is provided on this aggregator page. The most recent calls referenced (FISONG 2022, APCC 2022, FSOF) have no future deadlines stated; FISONG was suspended in 2023. No currently open call with a future deadline is identified.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page is an aggregator/index describing multiple financing mechanisms and past calls (FISONG 2018, 2020, 2021, 2022; APCC 2022; FSOF). No single currently-open call with a specific future deadline or application window is presented. FISONG was suspended in 2023.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The funding is explicitly targeted at CSOs/NGOs for international solidarity projects. The ILO, as an IGO, is not an eligible applicant under any of the mechanisms described.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The page describes grant/co-financing mechanisms for CSO projects, not procurement or consultancy tenders.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page states co-financing requirements: AFD provides up to 80% for French CSOs and up to 90% for local CSOs, implying mandatory co-financing of at least 10–20% from the applicant.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required; eligibility is based on organizational type and existence of more than three years.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No explicit rule requiring consortium leadership by a non-UN entity is stated, though only NGOs/CSOs are eligible as lead applicants.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: For the main CSO Initiatives Platform, eligible applicants must be French associations (under the Law of 1901), French foundations, or syndicates under French law, or local CSOs registered in DAC-eligible countries. Geographic restriction to French-registered entities applies for the primary funding stream.
